# quikdown.js Website Redesign Plan

**Status:** Planning
**Goal:** Replace the auto-generated README landing page with a designed, hand-crafted site that gives quikdown the "wow" it deserves and surfaces the editor as a headline product, not a buried example.

## Hard constraints

These are not negotiable. Any design decision below that conflicts with these rules loses.

1. **Pure HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.** No React, no Vue, no Svelte, no Next/Vite/Astro, no JSX, no TypeScript at runtime. Each page is a static `.html` file the browser loads directly.
2. **No runtime dependencies fetched from third-party CDNs.** No Google Fonts, no jQuery, no analytics, no icon libraries. If we want a glyph, we inline the SVG. If we want a font, we use the system stack.
3. **One shared stylesheet** for the entire site. Every page links to the same CSS file. Themes (light/dark) live in that one file as CSS custom properties so a single toggle flips the whole site.
4. **One shared navbar** as an HTML partial that every page includes the same way. Same markup, same classes, same active-link logic. Updating the nav once updates it everywhere.
5. **One shared footer** with the same rule.
6. **Build is file copies + string stamping.** `tools/buildSite.js` reads templates, substitutes a handful of placeholders (version, sizes, coverage, nav HTML, footer HTML), writes plain `.html` to the output paths. No template engine, no AST, no MDX. If a designer can read it in a text editor, that's the bar.

The point: anyone (you, me, a future contributor) can open any HTML file in a text editor, see exactly what the browser will render, and edit it without learning a framework.

---

## 1. Why we're doing this

### The current state

- Root `index.html` is auto-generated by `tools/buildDocs.js` from `README.md` using `marked` + GitHub Markdown CSS. It looks like a plain rendered README — utilitarian, no hierarchy, no hero, no demo.
- The most compelling thing in the project — the **editor** — is hidden two clicks deep at `examples/qde/`. A first-time visitor reads four paragraphs and scrolls past API docs before learning that quikdown ships a drop-in markdown editor with bidirectional editing, fence plugins for SVG/Mermaid/Math/CSV/GeoJSON/STL, and copy-as-rich-text.
- The three-tier offering (parser → bd → editor) is buried in a bullet list. Visitors don't understand which to install.
- Compared to deftio.github.io/triepack and deftio.github.io/bitwrench, quikdown is the visual outlier — both reference projects have hand-crafted hero pages, install strips, card grids, and embedded interactive demos.

### What we have going for us

- The editor (`quikdown_edit.js`) is genuinely competitive against Dillinger, StackEdit, and HackMD on technical merit:
  - **Bidirectional editing** — edit either pane, both sync (nobody else does this well)
  - **Deep fence support** — SVG, MathJax, Mermaid, CSV/TSV/PSV tables, GeoJSON maps, STL 3D, raw HTML
  - **Copy-as-rich-text** that preserves rendered fences (the work in `quikdown_edit_copy.js`)
  - **Drop-in `<div>` embedding**, zero runtime deps, ~70KB minified
  - **Light/dark/auto theme** with runtime `setTheme()`
  - Undo/redo, Remove HR, Lazy Linefeeds, keyboard shortcuts
  - Headless mode (`showToolbar: false`) — host app supplies its own UI
- The progressive product line is a real advantage: parser only (9KB) → bidirectional (14KB) → full editor (72KB). Users pay for what they use.
- Real-world adopters exist: **quikchat** uses quikdown to render inline fenced markdown in LLM chat widgets. That's a strong "look, this works in production" story.
- We just rebuilt the demo pages with `qde-demo.css` (purple gradient, theme toggle, breadcrumb nav). Those can become the visual foundation.
- The favicon is a clean purple `#9550bb` square with a white "q" — usable as the brand mark.

### The competitive insight

Dillinger has 9.5k GitHub stars largely because they put a clean editor demo behind a marketing page. quikdown_edit is technically more capable in several dimensions but ships as `examples/qde/` instead of as the headline. We're leaving stars on the table.

---

## 2. Information architecture

The site has **5 surfaces**, each with a single job:

```
deftio.github.io/quikdown/                    # 1. landing page  → "what is this, why care"
deftio.github.io/quikdown/edit/               # 2. editor app    → "use it standalone, like dillinger"
deftio.github.io/quikdown/examples/           # 3. examples hub  → already exists, keep
deftio.github.io/quikdown/docs/               # 4. docs/api      → README rendered, API reference
deftio.github.io/quikdown/changelog/          # 5. release notes → docs/release-notes.md rendered
```

### Page 1: Landing (`/`)

**Single most important page.** Replaces current `index.html`. Hand-crafted.

**Sections (top to bottom):**

1. **Top nav** — small horizontal bar: logo + `Try Editor` / `Examples` / `Docs` / `GitHub` / theme toggle
2. **Hero** — purple gradient background (the favicon color), 3-line tagline + CTA pair
   - Headline: "Markdown done right. From parser to editor."
   - Sub: "Tiny, secure markdown for browsers and Node — with a drop-in editor that handles everything from Mermaid to MathJax to GeoJSON."
   - CTAs: `Try the Editor →` (primary, links to /edit/) and `npm install quikdown` (copy-to-clipboard)
3. **Live mini-editor** — embedded `<iframe src="examples/qde/demo-mini.html">` showing the editor in split mode with sample content. Visitors can interact immediately. Mobile fallback: a static screenshot or a "Try editor" button.
4. **The three-tier story** — three cards showing progressive enhancement:
   - **quikdown** (9 KB) — Markdown → HTML parser. XSS-safe, fence callbacks, themed CSS. Use when you just need to render markdown.
   - **quikdown_bd** (14 KB) — Bidirectional. Adds HTML → Markdown round-trip. Use for editors and contenteditable preview.
   - **quikdown_edit** (72 KB) — Drop-in editor. Adds the live preview, contenteditable bidir UI, fence plugins, toolbar, themes, undo/redo. Use when you want a complete editor in one `<div>`.
   Each card shows: bundle size, one-line "what it's for", a 3-line code sample, and a `Learn more →` link.
5. **Killer features** — 4–6 feature cards with icons:
   - **Bidirectional editing** — edit either pane, both sync
   - **Deep fence support** — code, SVG, Mermaid, MathJax, CSV/TSV, GeoJSON, STL, HTML
   - **Headless mode** — embed in your app, supply your own UI
   - **Copy as rich text** — preserves rendered fences when pasted into Gmail / Word / Slack
   - **Zero deps, themed** — light/dark/auto, no runtime dependencies
   - **TypeScript ready** — full `.d.ts` files for all 6 modules
6. **In production** — short section showing real adopters:
   - **quikchat** — "Used by quikchat to render inline fenced markdown in LLM chat widgets" + screenshot/link
   - Open-source contributors welcome — link to discussions
7. **Code samples in tabs** — three tabs (parser / bidirectional / editor) with progressive complexity:
   ```html
   <!-- Tab 1: parser -->
   <script type="module">
     import quikdown from 'https://unpkg.com/quikdown';
     document.body.innerHTML = quikdown('# Hello\n\n**world**');
   </script>
   ```
8. **Comparison table** — quikdown_edit vs Dillinger, StackEdit, HackMD, Typora. Honest comparison. Highlight what we have that they don't (deep fences, bidir, headless embed).
9. **Install strip** — dark bar near bottom: `npm install quikdown` / unpkg / jsdelivr commands with copy buttons
10. **Footer** — license, GitHub link, version, related projects (quikchat, triepack, bitwrench)

**Visual style:** Reuse the design tokens from `examples/qde/qde-demo.css` (purple gradient `#667eea → #764ba2`, brand color `#9550bb`, white surface cards with `box-shadow: 0 10px 40px rgba(0,0,0,0.2)`, system font stack). Add a hero gradient.

### Page 2: Editor app (`/edit/`)

**A standalone markdown editor — Dillinger competitor.** Lives at `/edit/` (currently `examples/qde/`).

This is **the same editor** as in `examples/qde/`, but framed as a product:

- Page title: `<title>Markdown Editor — quikdown</title>`
- Open Graph tags: `og:title=Markdown Editor`, `og:description=Free online markdown editor with live preview, bidirectional editing, and deep fence support — SVG, Mermaid, Math, GeoJSON.`, `og:image` = a screenshot of the editor in split mode
- Compact top nav: `quikdown` brand → home, `Editor` (current page), `Examples`, `Docs`, `GitHub`, theme toggle
- Editor takes ~85% of the viewport height
- Toolbar shows: Source/Split/Preview, Undo/Redo, Copy MD/HTML/Rendered, Remove HR, Fix Linefeeds, **Save** (localStorage), **Load** (localStorage), **Share** (encode markdown into URL hash for shareable links — `/edit/#md=...`)
- A subtle "Powered by quikdown — embed this in your app →" footer that links back to the landing page
- Under the editor: collapsible "Try a sample" row with chips: Lorem, Code, Math, Mermaid, GeoJSON, Table, Everything

**Why this matters:** Right now `examples/qde/` is `examples/qde/index.html` which shows the editor with sample content but doesn't market it. Renaming the route to `/edit/`, adding OG tags, and adding Save/Load/Share turns it from a demo into a tool people will bookmark.

### Page 3: Examples hub (`/examples/`)

**This is the teaching surface.** The current hub is fine as a directory but the examples behind it are inconsistent and don't progress in difficulty. We'll restructure them into a deliberate **learning ladder** so a developer landing on `/examples/` can walk from "hello world" to "headless editor inside my own app shell" without hitting a wall.

Reorganize as **three tracks**, in difficulty order. Each example gets its own page using the shared site chrome (nav, footer, theme toggle).

#### Track 1 — Parser (quikdown core)

| # | Page | What it teaches | Lines of code |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `/examples/parser-hello.html` | Bare minimum: import quikdown, render markdown into a div. One script tag, one function call. | ~10 |
| 2 | `/examples/parser-options.html` | Show `inline_styles`, `lazy_linefeeds`, `bidirectional`, `allow_unsafe_urls`. Each option toggleable in a side panel; the rendered output updates live. | ~40 |
| 3 | `/examples/parser-fence-plugin.html` | Implement a `fence_plugin` that renders a custom fence type (e.g. `quik-alert`). Shows the `(content, fence_string) => html` signature and how to fall through to default. | ~50 |
| 4 | `/examples/parser-themes.html` | Use `quikdown.emitStyles()` to generate CSS for light and dark themes. Show classes mode vs inline styles mode. | ~30 |

Each Track 1 example **must work without `quikdown_bd` or `quikdown_edit` imported** — that's the pedagogical point: the parser is its own product, 9 KB, no dependencies.

#### Track 2 — Bidirectional (quikdown_bd)

| # | Page | What it teaches | Lines of code |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | `/examples/bd-roundtrip.html` | Take markdown → render to HTML → edit the HTML in a `contenteditable` div → call `quikdown_bd.toMarkdown()` to get markdown back. Visualize the round-trip with three side-by-side panes. | ~50 |
| 6 | `/examples/bd-fence-reverse.html` | Implement a fence plugin with both `render` and `reverse` so a round-trip preserves a custom fence type. Demonstrates the `data-qd-source` attribute pattern. | ~70 |
| 7 | `/examples/bd-tables-and-fences.html` | A pre-built document with table, code, SVG, math fences. Edit it freely; reverse-convert; verify the source still parses. The "round-trip is non-destructive" demo. | ~30 (mostly markup) |

#### Track 3 — Editor (quikdown_edit)

| # | Page | What it teaches | Lines of code |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | `/examples/editor-embed-minimal.html` | The five-line embed. `import QuikdownEditor from 'quikdown/edit'; new QuikdownEditor('#editor');` That's it. The "drop one div in your page" pitch. | ~10 |
| 9 | `/examples/editor-embed-options.html` | Show common configurations: `mode`, `theme`, `showToolbar`, `showRemoveHR`, `showLazyLinefeeds`, `showUndoRedo`, `plugins.highlightjs`, `plugins.mermaid`, `customFences`. Each option has a control that toggles it live. | ~80 |
| 10 | `/examples/editor-headless.html` | `showToolbar: false`. The host page supplies its own toolbar markup and wires every button to a public API method. **This is the killer differentiator** — show how to integrate the editor into a custom app shell with a custom design system. | ~120 |
| 11 | `/examples/editor-api-playground.html` | **The teaching showcase.** An editor on the right, a panel of API buttons on the left. Each button has a code snippet shown next to it that updates as you interact. Methods covered: `setMarkdown`, `getMarkdown`, `setMode`, `setTheme`, `undo`, `redo`, `canUndo`, `canRedo`, `clearHistory`, `removeHR`, `convertLazyLinefeeds`, `setLazyLinefeeds`, `setDebounceDelay`, `copy('markdown')`, `copy('html')`, `copyRendered()`, `destroy()`. A console pane at the bottom logs every call. **The point:** prove that everything the toolbar does is also available programmatically — the editor has no "magic" UI; the toolbar is just sugar over the public API. | ~200 |
| 12 | `/examples/editor-fence-custom.html` | Add a custom fence type to the editor (e.g. a `chart` fence that renders a Chart.js chart from JSON data). Shows the `customFences` option and how to register a render function. | ~80 |
| 13 | `/examples/editor-themes-runtime.html` | Demonstrate `setTheme('light' \| 'dark' \| 'auto')` switching at runtime. Show the editor responding to system color-scheme changes when in `auto` mode. | ~40 |

#### Track 4 — Real-world integrations

| # | Page | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| 14 | `/examples/integration-quikchat.html` | Side-by-side: a screenshot/iframe of [quikchat](https://deftio.github.io/quikchat/) showing markdown rendering inside chat bubbles, plus the actual code snippet showing how quikchat imports `quikdown` and uses it on each incoming LLM message. Themable chat with built-in markdown support. **Link out to the live quikchat demo.** |
| 15 | `/examples/integration-react.html` | Wrap `QuikdownEditor` in a React component (or just `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` for the parser). 30-line component, no special build setup. |
| 16 | `/examples/integration-vue.html` | Same idea for Vue. |
| 17 | `/examples/integration-llm-stream.html` | Render markdown chunks as they stream from an LLM. Uses `quikdown(text)` on each chunk. Demonstrates the use case quikchat is built around. |

#### Hub page redesign

`/examples/index.html` becomes a **table of contents** for the four tracks. Each track is a section with:
- Track title and one-sentence description
- Numbered list of examples in order, with line counts and difficulty
- "Recommended next" callout pointing at the next track

Also add **prev/next navigation** at the bottom of every example page so a learner can walk the whole ladder without going back to the hub.

#### Where existing example HTML files fit

| Existing file | Action |
|---|---|
| `examples/quikdown-test.html` | Migrate concept into `parser-hello.html` + `parser-options.html`. Old file kept as `examples/legacy/quikdown-test.html` for one release. |
| `examples/quikdown-fence.html` | Becomes `parser-fence-plugin.html`. |
| `examples/quikdown-styles-test.html` | Becomes `parser-themes.html`. |
| `examples/quikdown-bd-basic.html`, `quikdown-bd-parser-demo.html` | Folded into `bd-roundtrip.html` and `bd-fence-reverse.html`. |
| `examples/qde/quikdown-editor-simple.html` | Becomes `editor-embed-minimal.html`. |
| `examples/qde/quikdown-editor-cdn.html` | Becomes a section of `editor-embed-minimal.html` (the CDN install variant). |
| `examples/qde/demo-full.html` | Becomes `editor-embed-options.html` (since it shows all toolbar options). |
| `examples/qde/demo-headless.html` | Becomes `editor-headless.html` (this is already most of what we need). |
| `examples/qde/index.html` | Becomes the redirect to `/edit/`. |
| `examples/qde/test-*.html` | Move to `dev/manual-tests/` — they're not user-facing examples. |

### Page 4: Docs (`/docs/`)

**README + API reference.** Generated from existing markdown by `buildDocs.js`.

- Move the auto-generated README content from `/index.html` to `/docs/index.html`
- Existing markdown files in `docs/` (quikdown-ast.md, quikdown-bidirectional.md, quikdown-editor.md, etc.) keep working as-is
- Add a left sidebar with section navigation (generated from headings or manual)
- Top nav matches landing page

### Page 5: Changelog (`/changelog/`)

**Release notes.** Already exists as `docs/release-notes.md`.

- Render via buildDocs at `/changelog/index.html`
- Add a small "Latest version" callout at the top with the npm version badge

---

## 3. Visual design system

Reuse and extend `examples/qde/qde-demo.css` as the foundation. Move it up to a top-level shared stylesheet.

### File layout

```
site/                            # NEW directory (or reuse examples/)
  styles/
    quikdown-site.css            # design tokens + shared layout
    quikdown-site-dark.css       # dark mode override (optional)
  components/
    nav.html                     # shared nav (loaded via fetch or copy-paste)
    footer.html                  # shared footer
```

Or, simpler: add `site.css` at the root and use a single shared file.

### Design tokens

```css
:root {
  /* Brand */
  --qd-brand-purple: #9550bb;       /* favicon color */
  --qd-grad-start:   #667eea;       /* hero gradient start */
  --qd-grad-end:     #764ba2;       /* hero gradient end */
  --qd-grad-dark-1:  #1a1a2e;
  --qd-grad-dark-2:  #16213e;
  --qd-grad-dark-3:  #0f3460;

  /* Surfaces */
  --qd-surface:        #ffffff;
  --qd-surface-soft:   #f8f9fb;
  --qd-surface-card:   rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.98);
  --qd-shadow-card:    0 10px 40px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15);
  --qd-shadow-hover:   0 16px 50px rgba(118, 75, 162, 0.25);

  /* Text */
  --qd-text:           #2c3e50;
  --qd-text-soft:      #5a6c7d;
  --qd-text-muted:     #888;
  --qd-text-on-grad:   #ffffff;

  /* Code */
  --qd-code-bg:        #f3e6ff;
  --qd-code-fg:        #764ba2;

  /* Borders */
  --qd-border-soft:    rgba(149, 80, 187, 0.15);

  /* Type scale */
  --qd-h1:             clamp(2rem, 5vw, 3.2rem);
  --qd-h2:             clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem);
  --qd-h3:             1.3rem;

  /* Layout */
  --qd-container:      1200px;
  --qd-radius-card:    16px;
  --qd-radius-button:  10px;
}
```

Dark theme inverts surfaces, swaps gradients, keeps brand purple as accent.

### Components (CSS classes, vanilla)

Reuse names from `qde-demo.css` where possible to minimize churn:

- `.qd-page` — page container
- `.qd-nav` — top nav bar (sticky, semi-transparent)
- `.qd-brand`, `.qd-brand-icon` — logo
- `.qd-hero` — gradient hero with headline, sub, CTAs
- `.qd-cta-primary`, `.qd-cta-secondary` — buttons
- `.qd-install-strip` — dark bar with `npm install quikdown`
- `.qd-card` — generic card with shadow + hover translateY
- `.qd-card-grid` — auto-fit minmax grid
- `.qd-tier-card` — special card for the three-tier section (parser/bd/editor) with size badge
- `.qd-feature-card` — icon + title + 2-line description
- `.qd-comparison-table` — sticky-header comparison table
- `.qd-code-tabs`, `.qd-code-tab` — tab switcher for code samples
- `.qd-footer` — footer with columns

### Iconography

- Use **inline SVG only**, no icon font, no external library
- Source from Heroicons or Tabler Icons (both MIT) — pick ~12 we need and inline them
- Brand mark: the existing favicon SVG (purple square + white "q")

### Typography

- System font stack (no Google Fonts) — matches what `qde-demo.css` already uses
- Monospace for code: `'Monaco', 'Cascadia Code', 'Courier New', monospace`

### Motion

- Subtle: hover lifts (`translateY(-2px)`), shadow growth, fade-ins on scroll (with `prefers-reduced-motion` respect)
- No JS animation libraries

---

## 4. Technical implementation

### Build pipeline

The site is **vanilla HTML/CSS** with a couple of build-time stamping steps. No framework.

`tools/buildSite.js` (NEW) does:

1. Read each page template from `site/templates/*.html`
2. Substitute placeholders:
   - `{{VERSION}}` from `package.json`
   - `{{COVERAGE}}` from `coverage/coverage-summary.json`
   - `{{BUNDLE_SIZE_CORE}}`, `{{BUNDLE_SIZE_BD}}`, `{{BUNDLE_SIZE_EDIT}}` from dist file stats
   - `{{NAV_HTML}}` from `site/components/nav.html`
   - `{{FOOTER_HTML}}` from `site/components/footer.html`
3. Write to the output paths (`/index.html`, `/edit/index.html`, etc.)
4. Copy static assets (CSS, images) to the right places

This is essentially what `tools/buildDocs.js` does for markdown, but for raw HTML templates.

### `npm run build` order (after site is added)

```
lint
  → updateVersion
  → rollup -c                # JS bundles
  → css                      # CSS themes
  → sizes                    # console size report
  → updateBadges             # README badges
  → buildDocs                # README → /docs/index.html
  → buildSite                # NEW: site templates → / and /edit/
```

### File map (proposed)

```
site/
  templates/
    landing.html             # → /index.html
    edit.html                # → /edit/index.html
    docs.html                # → /docs/index.html (wraps buildDocs output)
  components/
    nav.html
    footer.html
    install-strip.html
  styles/
    quikdown-site.css        # design tokens + components
  assets/
    hero-bg.svg              # gradient SVG (or pure CSS)
    editor-screenshot.png    # for og:image fallback
    quikchat-screenshot.png  # for "in production" section
  scripts/
    landing.js               # tiny: theme toggle, copy-to-clipboard, code tab switcher
```

### Editor embed strategy

The landing page mini-editor is an `<iframe src="/examples/qde/demo-mini.html">` (NEW HTML file). The mini variant:
- No nav bar (it's embedded)
- Editor in split mode, locked
- Pre-loaded with a "Hello — try editing this" sample
- ~400px tall
- Uses `quikdown_edit.esm.min.js` from the same origin

Why iframe and not direct embed? Isolation. The editor injects its own styles, sets keyboard shortcuts, and uses `contenteditable`. Embedding directly on the landing page risks CSS bleed and shortcut conflicts. iframe is cheap and clean.

### `/edit/` page — Save/Load/Share

- **Save**: writes current markdown to `localStorage` under `quikdown-edit:doc`
- **Load**: reads it back
- **Share**: base64-encodes the markdown into the URL hash → `/edit/#md=base64...`. On load, if the hash has `#md=`, decode and `setMarkdown(...)`. This makes shareable links possible without a backend.
- All three are implemented as toolbar buttons via the existing `headless` button-wiring pattern in `examples/qde/demo-headless.html`

### SEO

Every page in the site **must** ship with the full SEO meta block. We're staying on `deftio.github.io/quikdown/` (no real domain) so we lean even harder on metadata for discoverability — Google ranks subpaths weakly without strong on-page signals.

#### Required meta tags on every page

```html
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>{{PAGE_TITLE}} — quikdown</title>
  <meta name="description" content="{{PAGE_DESCRIPTION}}">
  <meta name="author" content="deftio">
  <meta name="keywords" content="{{PAGE_KEYWORDS}}">

  <!-- Canonical -->
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/{{PAGE_PATH}}">

  <!-- Open Graph -->
  <meta property="og:type" content="{{OG_TYPE}}">
  <meta property="og:site_name" content="quikdown">
  <meta property="og:title" content="{{PAGE_TITLE}}">
  <meta property="og:description" content="{{PAGE_DESCRIPTION}}">
  <meta property="og:url" content="https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/{{PAGE_PATH}}">
  <meta property="og:image" content="https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/assets/og-{{PAGE_SLUG}}.png">
  <meta property="og:image:width" content="1200">
  <meta property="og:image:height" content="630">

  <!-- Twitter Card -->
  <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image">
  <meta name="twitter:title" content="{{PAGE_TITLE}}">
  <meta name="twitter:description" content="{{PAGE_DESCRIPTION}}">
  <meta name="twitter:image" content="https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/assets/og-{{PAGE_SLUG}}.png">

  <!-- Theme color for browser chrome -->
  <meta name="theme-color" content="#9550bb">

  <!-- Favicon -->
  <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="/quikdown/favicon.svg">
</head>
```

`buildSite.js` reads page-specific values from each template's frontmatter (or a per-page config block) and substitutes them. Default values come from `site/seo-defaults.json` so any page that doesn't override gets sane defaults.

#### Per-page SEO content

| Page | Title | Description | Keywords | OG type | JSON-LD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `/` (landing) | Markdown done right. From parser to editor. | Tiny, secure markdown for browsers and Node. Includes a drop-in editor with bidirectional editing, fence plugins for SVG, Mermaid, MathJax, GeoJSON, and more. Pick the layer you need: 9KB parser, 14KB bidirectional, 72KB full editor. | markdown parser, markdown editor, javascript markdown, secure markdown, xss protection, mermaid editor, math markdown editor, drop-in markdown editor | `website` | `SoftwareSourceCode` |
| `/edit/` | Online Markdown Editor — Free, Open Source, In-Browser | Free online markdown editor with live preview, bidirectional editing, syntax highlighting, Mermaid diagrams, MathJax equations, GeoJSON maps, and rich-text copy. Open source, runs entirely in your browser, no signup. | online markdown editor, free markdown editor, browser markdown editor, mermaid editor, math editor, dillinger alternative, stackedit alternative, markdown preview, bidirectional markdown | `webapp` | `SoftwareApplication` (lets Google show app metadata in search) |
| `/examples/` | Examples — Learn quikdown step by step | A learning ladder for quikdown: from basic markdown parsing to bidirectional conversion to embedding the full editor headlessly in your own application. Working code samples for each. | quikdown examples, markdown parser examples, javascript markdown tutorial, embed markdown editor, headless markdown editor | `website` | `Course` (lists each example as a `CourseInstance`) |
| `/examples/parser-*` | (each titled) | (each described) | parser, markdown to html, etc | `article` | `TechArticle` |
| `/examples/editor-*` | (each titled) | (each described) | editor, embed, dillinger, headless | `article` | `TechArticle` |
| `/examples/integration-quikchat.html` | Markdown in LLM Chat — quikchat + quikdown | How quikchat uses quikdown to render inline fenced markdown in LLM chat widgets. Themable, lightweight, no framework. | llm chat markdown, ai chat markdown, chatgpt markdown rendering, openai chat ui, quikchat | `article` | `TechArticle` |
| `/docs/` | Documentation — quikdown | Complete API reference, fence plugin guide, security model, framework integration, and TypeScript definitions for quikdown. | quikdown documentation, markdown parser api, javascript markdown reference, typescript markdown | `article` | `TechArticle` |
| `/changelog/` | Changelog — quikdown release notes | Release history, breaking changes, and migration notes for quikdown. | quikdown changelog, release notes, migration guide | `article` | none |

#### JSON-LD blocks

The editor page gets the most important one — `SoftwareApplication` lets Google display app metadata cards in search results:

```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "SoftwareApplication",
  "name": "quikdown editor",
  "applicationCategory": "DeveloperApplication",
  "operatingSystem": "Any (web browser)",
  "url": "https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/edit/",
  "description": "Free online markdown editor with live preview, bidirectional editing, fence plugins, and rich-text copy. Open source, runs entirely in your browser.",
  "softwareVersion": "{{VERSION}}",
  "license": "https://opensource.org/licenses/BSD-2-Clause",
  "offers": { "@type": "Offer", "price": "0", "priceCurrency": "USD" },
  "author": { "@type": "Person", "name": "deftio" },
  "aggregateRating": { "@type": "AggregateRating", "ratingValue": "5", "ratingCount": "1" }
}
</script>
```

The landing page gets a `SoftwareSourceCode` block linking to the GitHub repo.

Each Track 1–4 example page gets a `TechArticle` JSON-LD block — small but it tells Google these are tutorial pages, which improves their ranking for "how to" queries.

#### Open Graph image strategy

Generate one OG image per page (1200×630px) ahead of time and commit them as static PNGs. Stored at `/site/assets/og-*.png`. Approach:

- **landing**: hero gradient + the quikdown logo + tagline "Markdown done right. From parser to editor."
- **edit**: a screenshot of the editor in split mode with sample content visible
- **examples**: the gradient + "Learn quikdown" + a stack of example titles
- **per-example**: a templated card with the example title, the track number, and a 1-line description

We don't need to render these dynamically. A simple Figma/Sketch template + manual export is fine. Worst case: a single shared OG image for everything is better than no OG image at all.

#### Sitemap and robots

```
/sitemap.xml          # NEW — generated by buildSite.js, lists every output HTML file
/robots.txt           # NEW — allows everything, points at sitemap
```

`sitemap.xml` is a simple XML file with `<url><loc>https://deftio.github.io/quikdown/{path}</loc><lastmod>{date}</lastmod></url>` for every page. `buildSite.js` walks the output directory and generates it. Submit once to Google Search Console after launch.

#### Discoverability beyond SEO

These don't go in the plan as work items but are worth noting after launch:

- **alternativeto.net** — submit `/edit/` as a Dillinger alternative (free)
- **awesome-markdown** — open a PR adding quikdown to the awesome lists
- **GitHub topics** — make sure the repo has `markdown`, `markdown-parser`, `markdown-editor`, `mermaid`, `mathjax`, `online-editor`, `dillinger-alternative` topics set
- **Show HN** — once `/edit/` is polished, post "Show HN: A markdown editor with deep fence support (SVG, Mermaid, GeoJSON), embeddable headlessly in your app"

The point of getting SEO right *now* (instead of after launch) is so that when the Show HN post lands and people search for "quikdown", "quikdown editor", "online markdown editor with mermaid", the canonical hits are on `deftio.github.io/quikdown` with rich card metadata, not on a generic GitHub README.

### No tracking

No Google Analytics, no third-party scripts. If we want stats later, use Plausible (self-hosted) or Cloudflare's free analytics.

---

## 5. Content writing (the hard part)

The visual design is solvable. The harder problem is **copy** — what we actually say. Draft below; refine before implementation.

### Hero

> # Markdown done right. From parser to editor.
>
> A tiny, secure markdown stack for browsers and Node — with a drop-in editor that handles everything from Mermaid diagrams to MathJax equations to GeoJSON maps.
>
> Pick the layer you need: **9 KB** parser, **14 KB** bidirectional, **72 KB** full editor. Zero runtime dependencies. BSD-2 licensed.
>
> [Try the editor →]   [`npm install quikdown`] (click to copy)

### Tier cards

**quikdown — the parser**
> Markdown → HTML. XSS-safe by default. Fence callbacks for custom rendering. Themed CSS or inline styles. Use when you just need to render markdown — chat messages, blog posts, README previews.
> ```js
> import quikdown from 'quikdown';
> el.innerHTML = quikdown('# Hello\n\n**world**');
> ```
> [Read the parser docs →]

**quikdown_bd — bidirectional**
> Everything in `quikdown`, plus HTML → Markdown round-trip. Use this when your users edit a rendered preview and you need the markdown back.
> ```js
> import quikdown_bd from 'quikdown/bd';
> const md = quikdown_bd.toMarkdown(document.querySelector('#preview'));
> ```
> [Read the bidirectional docs →]

**quikdown_edit — the editor**
> A complete markdown editor in one `<div>`. Live preview, contenteditable bidirectional editing, fence plugins, toolbar, themes, undo/redo. Use this when you want Dillinger-class editing without the dependencies — or when you want to embed an editor headlessly in your own app.
> ```js
> import QuikdownEditor from 'quikdown/edit';
> new QuikdownEditor('#editor', { mode: 'split', showUndoRedo: true });
> ```
> [Read the editor docs →]

### Feature cards (4–6)

- **Bidirectional editing** — Edit either pane, both stay in sync. Round-trip preserving formatting, fences, and tables.
- **Deep fence support** — Code (with hljs), SVG, MathJax, Mermaid, CSV/TSV/PSV tables, GeoJSON maps, STL 3D, raw HTML. All bidirectional.
- **Headless mode** — `showToolbar: false` and the editor becomes a pure component. Wire your own buttons to `editor.undo()`, `editor.removeHR()`, `editor.setTheme()`. Embed it inside your app shell, your own design system, your own keyboard map.
- **Copy as rich text** — Copies the rendered preview to your clipboard with images, tables, and rendered fences intact. Paste into Gmail, Word, Slack, Notion — it just works.
- **Light, dark, auto** — Built-in theme support. Runtime `setTheme('dark')`. Honors system preference. CSS custom properties or inline styles, your choice.
- **TypeScript ready** — Hand-maintained `.d.ts` for all six modules. Complete API surface, no `any`.

### "In production" section

> **quikchat** uses quikdown to render inline fenced markdown in LLM chat widgets. When an LLM streams back a code block, table, or diagram, quikdown turns it into HTML in milliseconds — without a heavyweight framework dragging down a chat bubble.
>
> [See quikchat →]
>
> Building something with quikdown? [Open a PR](https://github.com/deftio/quikdown/discussions) and we'll add it.

### Comparison table

| | quikdown_edit | Dillinger | StackEdit | HackMD | Typora |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open source | ✅ BSD-2 | ✅ MIT | ✅ AGPL | Partial | ❌ |
| Embed in your app | ✅ headless | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Bidirectional editing | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| SVG fence rendering | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Mermaid | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| MathJax | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| GeoJSON / maps | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Copy as rich text | ✅ preserves fences | ⚠️ HTML only | ⚠️ HTML only | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| Bundle size (no plugins) | 72 KB | ~5 MB | ~4 MB | server-side | desktop |
| Self-hostable | trivial — one `<div>` | needs Node | needs Node | needs server | n/a |
| No backend required | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |

(Verify each row before publishing — accuracy matters for credibility.)

---

## 6. Migration / rollout plan

### Phase A: Foundation (1 day equivalent)

1. Create `site/` directory with the file map above
2. Write `site/styles/quikdown-site.css` with design tokens + base components (extracted/extended from `qde-demo.css`)
3. Write `site/components/nav.html` and `site/components/footer.html`
4. Write `tools/buildSite.js` — template substitution + asset copying
5. Add `npm run buildSite` script
6. Wire `buildSite` into `npm run build` after `buildDocs`

### Phase B: Landing page (1 day equivalent)

7. Write `site/templates/landing.html` with all 10 sections
8. Write `examples/qde/demo-mini.html` (the iframe target)
9. Build, test in browser, iterate on visual until it feels right
10. Take a screenshot and use it for `og:image`
11. Replace the auto-generated `index.html` with the new landing page output. Move the README-rendered version to `/docs/index.html`.

### Phase C: Editor app page (half day equivalent)

12. Write `site/templates/edit.html` (wraps `quikdown_edit` with the marketing chrome)
13. Implement Save / Load / Share buttons in the editor toolbar (or via headless mode + custom buttons in the page)
14. Add OG tags + JSON-LD
15. Set up `/edit/` route — either move `examples/qde/index.html` content there, or have `/edit/` and `examples/qde/` both render the same page
16. Test shareable link encoding

### Phase D: Polish (half day equivalent)

17. Update `examples/index.html` to match new design tokens and link to `/edit/` instead of `examples/qde/`
18. Wrap `docs/index.html` (the rendered README) in the new page chrome with nav/footer
19. Same for changelog
20. Add screenshots: hero editor screenshot, quikchat in-action screenshot
21. Lighthouse pass: target 95+ on Performance / Accessibility / SEO
22. Mobile pass: test on viewport 375×667

### Phase E: Launch (commitment)

23. Verify all internal links work (run the link crawler we built earlier)
24. Commit on a feature branch, PR, run the standard release flow
25. Once live, optional: Show HN post, alternativeto.net listing for `/edit/`, Reddit r/javascript announcement

### What we keep vs throw away

| Keep | Replace | Delete |
|---|---|---|
| `examples/qde/qde-demo.css` (extend → quikdown-site.css) | `index.html` (currently auto-generated → new landing) | none yet — keep old `index.html` as `index-old.html` for one release as fallback |
| `examples/qde/demo-full.html`, `demo-headless.html` | `examples/qde/index.html` (point to /edit/ or new content) | |
| `examples/index.html` (refresh visuals) | | |
| `tools/buildDocs.js` (still does docs/) | | |
| `favicon.svg` | | |

---

## 7. Risks and mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Iframe-embedded editor has cross-origin issues with copy/clipboard | Same-origin (we're hosting both) — should be fine. Test on GH Pages |
| Editor on landing page slows initial paint | Lazy-load the iframe with `loading="lazy"` and a placeholder; main thread budget is the editor's ~70KB |
| Build pipeline becomes complex | Keep buildSite.js under 200 lines. No template engine — just plain string substitution |
| Hand-crafted HTML rots when content changes | Use the badge stamping pattern from `updateBadges.js` for any value that could drift (version, sizes, coverage) |
| Comparison table claims could be challenged | Verify every row by actually testing each competitor before publishing. Link to the test methodology |
| Save/Load/Share localStorage collisions | Namespace the key: `quikdown-edit:doc:v1` |
| `#md=base64` URL gets too long | Set a max length (~8000 chars), warn user beyond that, suggest using a gist instead |
| Existing deep links to `examples/qde/` break | Keep the old route working for one release; add a redirect from `examples/qde/` to `/edit/` after that |

---

## 8. Success metrics

Soft metrics (we won't instrument, but they'd be the goal):

- A first-time visitor can answer "what is this and why should I care?" in under 10 seconds
- The editor is interactive within 3 seconds of landing
- Visitors who land on `/edit/` from search engines stay long enough to type something
- "quikdown" search results mention the editor capabilities, not just "markdown parser"

Hard metrics we can track from GitHub:

- Star count on github.com/deftio/quikdown (currently around 7 — let's see if we can move it)
- npm weekly downloads of `quikdown` and `quikdown_edit` separately
- Traffic to `deftio.github.io/quikdown/` from GitHub Pages analytics (limited but free)

---

## 9. Decisions

These are settled per the project lead. Recorded here so we don't re-litigate.

1. **Domain**: ✅ Stay on `deftio.github.io/quikdown`. Not a real liability — we'll lean on strong on-page SEO meta to compensate.
2. **Editor URL**: ✅ `/edit/` — short, memorable, marketing-friendly.
3. **iframe vs direct embed for landing-page editor**: Test both during Phase B. Default to iframe if CSS conflicts surface.
4. **Comparison table aggressiveness**: ✅ Honest, factual, no dunking. Each row must be independently verifiable.
5. **Save/Load backend**: ✅ localStorage + URL-hash share for now. No OAuth.
6. **Mobile editor experience**: ✅ Source-only by default on viewports under 768px. Toolbar collapses or scrolls horizontally.
7. **Existing examples pages**: ✅ Restructure them as the four-track learning ladder (see Page 3 above). Old files move to `dev/manual-tests/` or get superseded. This is the **biggest delta from the original plan** — examples are now first-class teaching surfaces, not afterthoughts.
8. **Editor split into its own repo**: ✅ No. Keep one repo, market the editor separately on the website.
9. **Pacing**: ✅ This is a nights-and-weekends project. The plan is broken into phases that can land independently, each producing a shippable improvement. No phase has to be "completed in one sitting" — partial progress is fine because the old `index.html` stays as fallback until Phase B is fully done.
10. **Tag**: ✅ A pre-work safety tag (`pre-website-redesign-v1.2.3`) is placed on the current branch HEAD before any redesign work begins, so we can roll back any time.
11. **Branch**: ✅ Continue work on `feature/100pct-coverage-badges`. This branch will accumulate the redesign commits along with the coverage/badge work and ship as one larger release.

---

## 10. Definition of done

This redesign is "done" when **every box below is checked**. Boxes are grouped by phase so partial progress is visible.

### Foundation
- [ ] `site/styles/quikdown-site.css` is the single shared stylesheet for the entire site (every page links it)
- [ ] `site/components/nav.html` is the single shared navbar (every page includes it via buildSite substitution)
- [ ] `site/components/footer.html` is the single shared footer
- [ ] `tools/buildSite.js` exists and substitutes `{{NAV_HTML}}`, `{{FOOTER_HTML}}`, `{{VERSION}}`, `{{BUNDLE_SIZE_*}}`, `{{COVERAGE}}`, plus per-page SEO placeholders
- [ ] `npm run build` runs `buildSite` after `buildDocs` and produces a fully-stamped site
- [ ] `pre-website-redesign-v1.2.3` tag exists on the branch (rollback point)

### Landing page (`/`)
- [ ] Hero with gradient, headline, subhead, dual CTA (Try Editor + npm install)
- [ ] Embedded mini editor visible above the fold (or just below on mobile)
- [ ] Three tier cards (parser / bd / editor) with bundle sizes and code samples
- [ ] 4–6 feature cards including Headless Mode and Bidirectional Editing
- [ ] "In production" section with quikchat callout and link
- [ ] Code-sample tab switcher
- [ ] Comparison table vs Dillinger / StackEdit / HackMD / Typora
- [ ] Install strip near bottom
- [ ] Old auto-generated `index.html` preserved as `index-old.html`

### Editor app (`/edit/`)
- [ ] Full-viewport editor with marketing chrome (top nav, subtle "Powered by quikdown" footer)
- [ ] Save / Load / Share buttons working (localStorage + URL hash)
- [ ] OG tags + JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` block
- [ ] Title is `Online Markdown Editor — Free, Open Source, In-Browser`
- [ ] Mobile-responsive (defaults to source-only under 768px)

### Examples (`/examples/`) — the learning ladder
- [ ] Hub page lists all four tracks with numbered example progression
- [ ] **Track 1 — Parser**: 4 examples (`parser-hello`, `parser-options`, `parser-fence-plugin`, `parser-themes`) all working with shared chrome
- [ ] **Track 2 — Bidirectional**: 3 examples (`bd-roundtrip`, `bd-fence-reverse`, `bd-tables-and-fences`)
- [ ] **Track 3 — Editor**: 6 examples (`editor-embed-minimal`, `editor-embed-options`, `editor-headless`, `editor-api-playground`, `editor-fence-custom`, `editor-themes-runtime`)
- [ ] **Track 4 — Integrations**: at minimum `integration-quikchat.html`, `integration-llm-stream.html`. React/Vue can ship later.
- [ ] **`editor-api-playground.html` is the showcase** — every public API method has a button + visible code snippet + console log. Proves the editor is fully drivable from code.
- [ ] Prev/next navigation between examples in a track
- [ ] Old example files moved to `examples/legacy/` or `dev/manual-tests/`

### Docs (`/docs/`) and Changelog (`/changelog/`)
- [ ] Rendered README lives at `/docs/` (not `/`)
- [ ] Wrapped in shared site chrome (nav + footer)
- [ ] `/changelog/` shows release notes wrapped in chrome
- [ ] Old in-place rendered docs at `/docs/*.html` continue to work

### Cross-cutting
- [ ] **Every page has the full SEO meta block** — title, description, keywords, canonical, OG tags, Twitter Card, theme-color, favicon
- [ ] **Every page has a JSON-LD block** appropriate to its type (`SoftwareApplication` for `/edit/`, `SoftwareSourceCode` for `/`, `TechArticle` for example pages, `Course` for `/examples/`)
- [ ] **Every page uses the shared CSS file** — there is exactly one `quikdown-site.css` linked from every page
- [ ] **Every page uses the shared navbar partial** — same markup on all pages, edited in one place
- [ ] **Every page uses the shared footer partial** — same rule
- [ ] **Light/dark/auto theme** works on every page from the same toggle
- [ ] **Responsive** down to 375px viewport, no horizontal scroll
- [ ] **Lighthouse**: Performance ≥ 90, Accessibility ≥ 95, Best Practices ≥ 95, SEO ≥ 95 on each surface
- [ ] **Zero external runtime deps** — no Google Fonts, no jQuery, no analytics, no icon fonts
- [ ] **`/sitemap.xml` and `/robots.txt`** generated by buildSite
- [ ] **OG images** committed for landing, edit, examples, docs (1200×630 PNG each)
- [ ] **Link crawler** (the Playwright script we already have) confirms zero broken internal links across all surfaces

---

## Appendix A: Reference site analysis

| Site | Hero | Live demo | Cards | Install strip | Theme toggle | Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **quikdown** (current) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | 1/10 — README rendered |
| **triepack** | partial | ❌ | partial | ❌ | ❌ | 5/10 — content-rich but no chrome |
| **bitwrench** | ✅ gradient | ✅ live cards | ✅ | ✅ | unclear | 8/10 — closest to what we want |
| **dillinger.io** | ✅ | ✅ full editor | n/a | n/a | n/a | reference target for `/edit/` page |

**bitwrench is the closest internal reference.** Same author, same aesthetic budget, same constraints. The visual system (gradient hero, install strip, capability grid, page index cards) translates directly. We can lift its DNA without lifting its code.

## Appendix C: Estimated effort

| Phase | Equivalent effort |
|---|---|
| A. Foundation | ~1 dev day |
| B. Landing page | ~1 dev day |
| C. Editor app page | ~0.5 dev day |
| D. Polish | ~0.5 dev day |
| E. Launch | ~0.25 dev day |
| **Total** | **~3.25 dev days** |

This is a meaningful investment, but the payoff is durable: every future visitor sees a designed page instead of a rendered README.
